FOMO-driven entries have been rare, with only a handful of cases over the past year. One example was $AAOI after Q1 earnings around the $40 level, which saw a rapid re-rating shortly after. In most cases, patience tends to matter more, as capital rotates while new setups form underneath crowded narratives. While attention is currently on humanoid robotics, recent market strength has been concentrated in energy-related names, with $FCEL and $BE both moving higher. Different approaches exist, from deep thematic analysis to shorter-term news-driven positioning.

$AAOI is getting renewed attention after Rosenblatt highlighted the optical theme and potential customer expansion in CPO. The note suggests $AMD could be the first CPO customer, with $NVDA and hyperscalers potentially following over time. 
The core thesis remains around accelerating demand for optical interconnect and potential share gains if execution aligns with the cycle. The setup is more about forward expectations and AI infrastructure scaling than near-term fundamentals.

Rosenblatt sees $AAOI as a top optical networking pick, citing potential demand from next-generation CPO deployments. The firm believes AMD could become AAOI's first CPO customer, with additional opportunities from other AI infrastructure players over time. Combined with company-specific growth drivers, the outlook points to improving market share and earnings momentum.

$AAOI serves as a powerful reminder of a core market truth in 2026: conviction is nothing without proper position sizing.  
 
Locking in the structural entry at the $82 support zone was an excellent tactical decision, but leaving money on the table by under-sizing remains a key lesson.  
 
When macro fundamentals align with a clean breakout setup, maximum capitalization is required.  
 
Great execution, even better risk-management takeaway.

$AAOI Applied Optoelectronics 
 
That’s a classic convex outcome trade: right entry + strong trend, but under-sizing the position limits the real impact of being right. 
 
What stands out in setups like this is exactly that tension between conviction and allocation — most PnL dispersion doesn’t come from being early or late, but from how much risk is actually expressed when the thesis is right. 
 
At $82, the asymmetry was clearly there. The real lesson isn’t the entry, it’s scaling into strength instead of letting it run without participation.

Coherent Corp. (NYSE: COHR) does not manufacture raw Indium Phosphide (InP) blank substrates. Instead, they act as an end-to-end device and optical chip manufacturer.Coherent purchases blank InP substrates from external specialized suppliers (such as AXT Inc.) and then applies their own epitaxy to manufacture finished optical components like lasers and photodiodes.

$AAOI got dragged down hard with the whole photonics group, but when you strip the noise away, this looks more like a positioning flush than a broken story. 
 
You had co-packaged optics delay fears, Fed turning more hawkish, memory names rolling over, and high-beta names all getting de-risked at the same time - everything just hit at once. 
 
At around $130, you’re basically paying ~2x the company’s mid-2027 revenue run-rate target (~$5.6B annualized). On the surface that sounds rich, but that’s only true if you assume growth slows down meaningfully from here. 
 
Right now the numbers still say otherwise - triple-digit growth profile, FY guidance around $1.1B, Q1 up 51%, over $300M+ in locked 800G/1.6T orders, and a pretty aggressive capacity buildout that’s expected to scale massively into 2027. Even 1.6T shipments are starting this summer. 
 
Yes, execution risk is real. This is not a perfect management story. But the tape is pricing it like demand is gone… while backlog and supply expansion are saying the opposite. 
 
That disconnect is usually where the most interesting setups come from.

$AAOI is definitely back on radar after that ~40% drawdown, but this is exactly the kind of setup where you separate narrative from confirmation. 
 
Yes, it’s sitting into a prior support / demand zone, and in names like optical / photonics, those 30–50% retracements are not unusual after strong momentum legs. The key issue is timing the actual base, not just identifying where it should base. 
 
That $121–$134 region only matters if price starts showing real stabilization there: tighter ranges, volume drying up on dips, and eventually a reclaim of lost moving averages. Without that, it’s just a falling knife zone, not confirmed support. 
 
The longer-term thesis in $AAOI still lives or dies on AI interconnect demand and hyperscaler capex, but the market will demand proof after a drawdown like this. 
 
As for $280+ targets, those only become relevant again once the market proves a new uptrend structure. Until then, it’s a watch-the-base, not a chase-the-projection situation.

Coherent (COHR) does not manufacture the raw Indium Phosphide (InP) crystal substrates from scratch. Instead, the process is divided:Sourcing: Coherent relies on specialized external suppliers (like AXT or Japanese vendors) to purchase the raw InP blank substrate wafers.Manufacturing: Coherent then takes these raw wafers into its own facilities—such as the one in Sherman, Texas—and performs advanced epitaxy and device fabrication to turn them into finished optical chips, lasers, and transceivers used for AI infrastructure.
There are only two companies: Axti and smtoy that produce highest grade InP raw materials.

A 6.4T optical engine uses more (InP) materials than a 3.2T optical engine.Why 6.4T Consumes More InP MaterialsThe primary driver behind InP material consumption is the total lane count and light source volume required to double the bandwidth from 3.2 Tbps to 6.4 Tbps:Lane Count Multiplication: To scale up to multi-terabit architectures, optical engines rely on multiplying physical lanes or laser wavelengths. A 6.4T engine requires twice the data-carrying capacity of a 3.2T engine. Even when transitioning to next-generation individual 400G-per-lane speeds, a 6.4T engine requires 16 channels, whereas a 3.2T engine requires only 8 channels.Laser Component Volume: Whether the architecture uses discrete InP Electro-absorption Modulated Lasers (EMLs) or continuous-wave (CW) InP lasers arrayed to feed a Silicon Photonics (SiPho) engine, the physical number of laser chips or array sizes must scale up to deliver the necessary optical power and channels.

The AI transition from training to inference is where things start to get interesting, but it’s not the obvious stuff like GPUs or even optics anymore. 
 
Every step from 400G to 800G to 1.6T isn’t just about bandwidth going up. It quietly makes synchronization way harder. More GPUs talking to each other, more optical links, more switching points… and suddenly timing errors start to matter a lot more than people think. 
 
Inference clusters especially are getting hit with way more timing content demand than training ever saw, sometimes 2–4x depending on system design. 
 
Everyone’s watching the obvious names like $AAOI, $COHR, $LITE… optical modules, lasers, transceivers, all that visible stuff. 
 
But the interesting part is the layer underneath. Every time these systems scale, someone is getting paid just for keeping everything in sync. 
 
Feels like the hidden AI tax isn’t optics at all… it’s timing. $SITM

$AAOI $COHR $CSCO $LITE A 6.4T  engine uses more Indium Phosphide (InP) materials than a 3.2T engine.Why 6.4T Consumes More InP MaterialsThe primary driver behind InP material consumption is the total lane count and light source volume required to double the bandwidth from 3.2 T per second  to 6.4 Tbps:Lane Count Multiplication: To scale up to multi-terabit architectures, optical engines rely on multiplying physical lanes or laser wavelengths. A 6.4T engine requires twice the data-carrying capacity of a 3.2T engine. Even when transitioning to next-generation individual 400G-per-lane speeds, a 6.4T engine requires 16 channels, whereas a 3.2T engine requires only 8 channels.Laser Component Volume: Whether the architecture uses discrete InP Electro-absorption Modulated Lasers (EMLs) or continuous-wave (CW) InP lasers arrayed to feed a Silicon Photonics (SiPho) engine, the physical number of laser chips or array sizes must scale up to deliver the necessary optical power and channels.

$AAOI up again today and still one of the most debated names in the photonics / AI interconnect space. 
 
This used to be a quiet legacy cable supplier, now it’s sitting right in the middle of AI bandwidth demand and cluster scaling narratives. That shift alone explains a lot of the volatility and disagreement around it. 
 
What I keep coming back to is how unusual the setup is. They’re vertically integrated on laser chips, which gives them a different supply dynamic than peers, but at the same time revenue is still heavily concentrated, so the risk cuts both ways. 
 
Even the dilution story gets oversimplified - it’s more about funding scale than just “bad capital structure.” 
 
And then you’ve got a longer-term roadmap pushing toward multi-terabit interconnect that the market is still debating how to price. 
 
It’s not a clean story, but it’s a high beta one. You size it, you don’t marry it.

$AXTI A 6.4T optical engine uses more Indium Phosphide (InP) materials than a 3.2T optical engine.Why 6.4T Consumes More InP MaterialsThe primary driver behind InP material consumption is the total lane count and light source volume required to double the bandwidth from 3.2 Terabits per second  to 6.4 Tbps:Lane Count Multiplication: To scale up to multi-terabit architectures, optical engines rely on multiplying physical lanes or laser wavelengths. A 6.4T engine requires twice the data-carrying capacity of a 3.2T engine. Even when transitioning to next-generation individual 400G-per-lane speeds, a 6.4T engine requires 16 channels, whereas a 3.2T engine requires only 8 channels.Laser Component Volume: Whether the architecture uses discrete InP Electro (EMLs) or continuous-wave (CW) InP lasers arrayed to feed a Silicon Photonics (SiPho) engine, the physical number of laser chips or array sizes must scale up to deliver the necessary optical power and channels.

INP SUBSTRATES - QUICK CLARIFICATION THREAD 
 
A few important points on the InP supply chain: 
 
• $AXTI is a key upstream player in InP substrates 
- has been getting permits to ship to U.S. companies operating outside the U.S. 
- still a critical “gate” supplier in the ecosystem 
 
• $COHR  - works with ~5 substrate suppliers (not single-sourced) 
                  - diversification is preferred for visibility + risk control 
 
• $LITE   - has long-term supply secured via Sumitomo 
               - still values multi-source optionality 
 
• $LITE / $COHR / $AAOI / $NOK - do NOT manufacture InP substrates 
   - they source substrates (AXT / others) and then build devices 
 
• Key distinction: 
“InP production / capacity” ≠ “InP substrates” 
 
- Device makers expand lasers, photonics, modulators, etc. 
- Substrate makers actually grow the raw material wafer supply 
 
The bottleneck sits upstream in substrates, while most headlines refer to downstream device expansion.

🚨The GPU gets all the attention. Almost nobody understands the stack that moves the data between them.

Three companies, three layers of the exact same trade.🧵

🟡 $SIVEF - the light source. Indium-phosphide DFB lasers. As AI moves to co-packaged optics, the laser sits outside the switch as an external source and someone has to make it. Smallest, earliest, purest photonics bet.

🟠 $AAOI - the module. Takes the lasers and optics and builds the 800G/1.6T transceiver and makes its own lasers in-house. Explosive growth, but hardware economics (~30–40% margins).

🟢 $CRDO - the interconnect. Copper active cables plus the retimers and SerDes that keep high-speed links clean. Copper for short reach, enabling optical for long reach. Chip economics (~65% margins)

Light source → module → interconnect. Owning all three isn’t redundant -> it’s owning the laser, the box, and the wire. Three layers, three different failure modes.

$CRDO 

$CRDO built its name on copper connectivity -> the SerDes, DSPs, and active electrical cables that move data between AI chips. The one open question was always what happens when the industry shifts from copper to optical. With the DustPhotonics acquisition, Credo brought silicon photonics in-house and now owns the full connectivity stack: SerDes → DSP → active cables → optical transceivers → silicon photonics, spanning 800G to a 3.2T roadmap. Electrical and optical, end to end.

That's the moat. When Jensen says the future of AI depends on connectivity, this is the layer he means and $CRDO is the pure-play that owns all of it.

$RPI being up close to ~3x is honestly not that unusual in these kinds of cycles 😅📊 
 
What gets labeled as “meme stocks” is often just a mix of liquidity, sentiment, and delayed fundamental repricing. ⚡ 
 
Looking back at the names you mentioned: 
$AXTI / $RPI / $IQE / $EWY / $SNDK / $AAOI / $SOI 
 
many of them tend to look underwhelming during compression phases, which naturally makes them easy to underestimate. 
 
But markets have a pretty consistent pattern: 
 
when sentiment is weakest, narratives suppress price the most; 
when institutions start repricing risk, moves can become extremely asymmetric. 
 
The “paper hands vs holding” debate is really just about time horizon and tolerance. 
 
Not everyone can sit through volatility long enough for repricing to play out. 
 
So in many cases, people don’t lose because of the stock — 
 
they lose because of timing 😏

SPACE BASED DATA CENTER: 

GOOGLE and PLANET LAB partnership and Google has 10% stake

The market may still be underestimating what this partnership could become. This is no longer just satellite imagery. The thesis is evolving toward orbital AI infrastructure.

Key idea:
AI is creating an energy and compute crisis on Earth.
Traditional data centers are running into:

* Power limitations
* Cooling constraints
* Land scarcity
* Grid bottlenecks
* Environmental pressure

Google’s “Project Suncatcher” is exploring solar-powered AI compute clusters in orbit using TPUs and satellite constellations.  

Why this matters for Planet:
Planet already has one of the largest operational satellite fleets and years of real-world experience managing constellations at scale.  

That gives Planet something incredibly valuable:

* Space infrastructure expertise
* Orbital operations experience
* Existing manufacturing capability
* Satellite fleet coordination
* Data transmission know-how

If orbital compute becomes real, Google may not want to build the operational layer alone.

Planet could evolve from:
“satellite imaging company”
into:
“AI infrastructure partner in orbit.”

The bigger narrative:
This is potentially the beginning of distributed space-based computing.

Instead of giant centralized terrestrial data centers:

* Compute nodes in orbit
* Near-continuous solar power
* Optical interconnect between satellites
* AI processing directly in space
* Real-time analytics before data even reaches Earth

That changes the economics of:

* Defense
* Climate monitoring
* Autonomous systems
* Global surveillance
* Disaster response
* Military intelligence
* Earth observation
* Space communications

Google itself acknowledged the concept involves constellations of AI satellites communicating together as compute clusters.  

Another overlooked angle:
Planet already generates enormous amounts of Earth data daily and has long relied on Google Cloud infrastructure for storage and processing.  

So the relationship is not new.
This has been building for years:

* Google sold Terra Bella to Planet
* Google became an investor
* Planet integrated deeply into Google Cloud
* Now they are collaborating on orbital AI compute research

That progression looks strategic rather than random.  

The asymmetric bull case:
If Project Suncatcher works even partially, Planet may gain exposure to:

$KOPN $POET $LITE $AAOI 

The Traditional Optical Bottleneck

Today’s AI infrastructure relies heavily on laser-based optical connectivity:

* External laser modules
* Complex silicon photonics packaging
* High power consumption
* Thermal management challenges
* Expensive scaling at massive GPU cluster levels

As AI clusters move toward millions of GPUs, the industry faces a growing problem:

moving data efficiently is becoming just as important as compute itself.

Traditional optical systems work well, but they were largely designed for telecom/datacenter evolution — not necessarily for hyperscale AI inference fabrics requiring ultra-low latency, extreme density, and lower power per bit transferred.

⸻

The Bullish KOPN Narrative: “MicroLED Optics”

The speculative bull thesis around KOPN is that MicroLED technology could evolve from displays into optical communication engines.

Instead of relying entirely on conventional laser transmitters, MicroLED arrays could potentially:

* transmit optical signals with lower power
* reduce heat generation
* enable tighter chip-to-chip connectivity
* allow denser optical packaging
* improve scalability for AI clusters

This creates the narrative of:

“optical interconnect reimagined for the AI era.”

Why Bulls Think This Matters

1. Power Efficiency

Traditional laser optics consume meaningful power at scale.

If MicroLED optical links can lower energy per transmitted bit, hyperscalers may see:

* lower electricity costs
* reduced cooling requirements
* higher rack density

In AI datacenters, even small efficiency gains become massive at hyperscale.

2. Smaller Form Factor

Laser-based systems often require:

* external modulators
* complex alignment
* larger optical assemblies

MicroLED architectures could theoretically integrate more directly with:

* GPUs
* AI accelerators
* co-packaged optics
* chiplets

That means:

shorter communication paths and potentially faster data movement.

3. AI Inference Explosion

Training AI is enormous — but inference may become even larger over time.

Inference workloads require:

* low latency
* high bandwidth
* constant GPU-to-GPU communication

Bulls believe future inference clusters may demand entirely new interconnect approaches beyond conventional optics.

This is where the “next-generation optical fabric” narrative emerges for KOPN.

Why the Market Could Re-rate It

The speculative excitement comes from this possibility:

If investors begin viewing KOPN not as:

* a legacy defense display company

but instead as:

* an AI optical infrastructure platform,

then valuation multiples could change dramatically.

That is what happened historically with several AI infrastructure names:

* companies moved from “small niche hardware suppliers”
* into “critical AI ecosystem enablers”

Once a company becomes associated with:

* AI connectivity
* optical infrastructure
* power-efficient scaling
* hyperscaler adoption

the market often begins pricing future TAM (total addressable market), not current revenue.

⸻

The Ultra-Bull Case

The most aggressive bull thesis is:

AI eventually hits a wall where traditional copper and even conventional optics become too power hungry and thermally inefficient.

If MicroLED optical interconnects prove viable at scale:

* AI clusters could become denser
* GPU communication faster
* inference cheaper
* datacenter power usage more efficient

In that scenario, KOPN would no longer compete as a display company — it would compete in the future AI interconnect market alongside photonics and optical infrastructure players.

⸻

Important Reality Check

This remains a forward-looking and speculative narrative:

* commercial adoption is not yet proven at hyperscale
* traditional laser optics are deeply entrenched
* execution risk is very high
* scaling manufacturing and reliability are critical hurdles

But the reason some investors are excited is because:

the upside of becoming part of next-generation AI connectivity infrastructure could be enormous relative to Kopin’s current size.

Massive Growth Potential for KOPN. 2 of the Top 5 Semiconductor interested with their AI optical interconnectivity:

OPTICAL INTERCONNECT VS KOPN MICROLED

1. Optical Interconnect (traditional)

This is the current standard in high-speed data systems.

Uses lasers (usually VCSELs or silicon photonics) to transmit data as light

Light travels through optical fiber or waveguides

Common in:
    * Data centers
    * AI clusters (think NVIDIA GPUs)
    * Telecom networks

Key idea:

Convert electrical → optical → electrical

Pros:

* Very fast (Tbps scale)
* Long distance capability
* Mature ecosystem

Cons:

* Power-hungry (especially conversions)
* Expensive packaging
* Heat + latency from multiple components

2.  MicroLED Interconnect (emerging concept)

This is newer and more experimental.

* Uses MicroLED arrays as the light source for data transmission
* Instead of a laser, each tiny LED pixel can transmit data
* Often paired with direct chip-to-chip or short-range optical links

Key idea:

Use massively parallel light emitters (MicroLED pixels) for communication

3. Why MicroLED interconnect matters

It’s trying to solve a growing bottleneck in AI systems:

* Moving data is becoming more expensive than computing

* Chips (GPUs, ASICs) are packed tightly → copper struggles

* Optical helps, but lasers + packaging = costly

MicroLED interconnect aims to:

* Reduce power consumption
* Enable short-range, ultra-dense data transfer
* Potentially integrate directly onto chips

Why KOPN MicroLed AI Optical Interconnect breakthrough caught my attention:

1. Data movement is becoming costlier than computation. Moving information now creates major burdens in power use, heat, latency, and packaging complexity. Using light for communication could reduce the hardware needed between endpoints.

2. It addresses the power bottleneck in dense computing. A large amount of energy is spent pushing bits over short copper links. Optical movement of data could lower power consumption, reduce cooling needs, and enable higher compute density per rack.

3. It shifts where industry value is created. Traditional interconnect ecosystems rely on modules, connectors, fiber, cages, retimers, and board-level components. Optical wireless links could shift value toward companies controlling emitters, receivers, and system integration.

4. It brings optics inside the system chassis.
Instead of optics only being used for external fiber connections, this concept suggests an internal optical fabric where components communicate directly using light without cables.

5. The technical challenge is extremely high.
Success requires ultra-fast microLED switching, precise receivers, beam control optics, stable alignment under heat/vibration, and reliable mass-scale manufacturing.

6. Interconnect is becoming central to computing architecture. As compute scales higher, the ability to move data efficiently becomes as important as processors themselves.

7. Any real breakthrough would attract major interest. A practical solution that moves bits with lower power and lower complexity could create significant industry and investor attention, even in early stages.

KOPM MicroLed Neural I/o™ will be designed to achieve the same functional outcome with a fraction of the power by using photons instead of electrons to move data, eliminating copper interconnects and expensive laser-based systems entirely. The architecture uses each MicroLED pixel as a high-speed transmitter, sending digital bits at extremely fast rates and enabling real-time GPU-to-GPU data exchange at massive scale.

WHAT IS THE DIFFERENCE: 

Traditional Optical interconnect Versus KOPN Microled Optical interconnect, the very first one: 

1. Current Optical Interconnect (Traditional)

Most today’s optical interconnect systems use:

* VCSELs (Vertical-Cavity Surface-Emitting Lasers)
* Silicon photonics with external lasers
* Electro-absorption modulators
* Fiber optics + transceivers

How it works:

Electrical signals from chips are converted into laser light, sent through fiber, then converted back into electrical signals.

Limitations:

* Higher power consumption
* Heat generation from lasers
* More expensive packaging
* Alignment complexity
* Scaling challenges for shorter-reach chip-to-chip links
* Latency from extra conversion steps

⸻

2. MicroLED Optical Interconnect

Uses tiny MicroLED emitters directly as ultra-fast light sources for data transmission instead of lasers.

How it works:

Electrical signals directly drive MicroLED arrays that emit modulated light into waveguides/fiber/free-space links.

Advantages:

Lower Power Consumption

MicroLEDs can be more energy efficient than lasers for short-reach links.

Lower Heat

Less thermal load than laser-based systems.

Smaller Size / Higher Density

Thousands of emitters can be packed into arrays.

Faster Chip Integration

Potential direct integration onto packages, AI accelerators, GPUs, data centers.

Lower Cost at Scale

Semiconductor-style manufacturing may reduce cost.

Ideal for Short-Reach High Bandwidth

Rack-to-rack, chip-to-chip, board-to-board.

⸻

3. Why This Matters for AI/Data Centers

Current copper links are hitting power and distance limits. Traditional optics help, but MicroLED optics could become the next generation because it may offer:

* Lower watts per bit
* Higher bandwidth density
* Better thermal profile
* Easier co-packaged optics design

⸻

4. Simple Analogy

* Traditional optical interconnect: Uses laser engines.
* MicroLED optical interconnect: Uses ultra-tiny LED engine arrays.

⸻

5. Current Reality

Traditional optical interconnect is already commercialized at scale.
MicroLED optical interconnect is emerging / next-wave technology, not yet dominant.

If successful, it could disrupt parts of the optical transceiver market.

⸻

6. Bottom Line

Traditional optics = established leader today.
MicroLED optics = potentially cheaper, cooler, denser future solution for short-distance AI compute connections.

BZAI technology focuses on Physical or edge AI:

    Edge AI → intelligence runs close to the data (device/local server)

* Cloud AI → intelligence runs far away in massive data centers 

AI-specific comparison (this is where it gets interesting)

Edge AI

* Runs on:
    * Phones
    * Cameras
    * IoT sensors
    * Autonomous vehicles
* Example:
    * Self-driving car braking instantly
    * Face recognition on a security camera

✔ Strengths:

* Real-time decisions (milliseconds)
* Privacy (data never leaves device)
* Works even offline

Edge AI dominates when:

* Real-time is critical
* Connectivity is unreliable
* Privacy matters

Examples:

* Autonomous driving
* Industrial robotics
* Smart camerasM

Bottom line

* Edge AI = fast, local, efficient
* Cloud AI = powerful, centralized, scalable
* Future = edge + cloud working together

Applied Optoelectronics rose on Friday after Citron Research disclosed a short position in the company. Citron contrasted AAOI with Lumentum, noting AAOI’s sharp rally from $85 to $140 in two weeks, adding about $3.5B in market cap following a press release.

$SOI pulling back 6% today is worth addressing directly because nothing about the underlying thesis has changed.
Soitec’s monopoly position over silicon photonics and CPO substrates is a structural reality, not a price action story. What has changed is the macro environment. The Paris Stock Index is down 9% this month and continues to deteriorate, with broader geopolitical pressure from the Iran conflict dragging down names indiscriminately. That context matters when interpreting short term moves.
A few important principles worth keeping in mind across all positions mentioned here.
Price volatility is not thesis invalidation. $AAOI correcting after a 400% run is normal market behavior. $SIVE dropping 16% on a Friday before gaining 35% on Monday illustrates exactly how misleading short term price action can be when the underlying fundamentals remain intact. These are not straight line stories no strong outperformer ever is.

the special case of $AAOI and Timeframes 
 
The stock showed a Bearish shooter bar on Weekly chart so is that an automatic sell? Normally that's a "Yes" but a better question might be: "what's your timeframe??" 
 
I bought the stock based on the Yearly chart so I'm ignoring the Weekly signals. Will I have some draw-down because of this? Yes of course but I am bullish and the position is still profitable so to me it's no biggie 
 
If you haven't established the Timeframe you are trading on it could lead to bigger mistakes $STUDY 
 
To Recap: 
The Weekly chart is a bearish Shooter 
The Monthly chart is a Box Breakout 
The Quarterly chart had two Box breakouts 
The Yearly chart is a Box breakout

Why AEHR is super important with Optics Photonic industry:

Aehr Test Systems plays a surprisingly important—but often overlooked—role in the optical/photonics supply chain, especially as data centers, AI, and high-speed networking explode

This matters because:
 • Photonics chips are very sensitive to defects
 • Failures in optical networks (data centers, telecom) are catastrophic and expensive

So companies need to:
 • Detect early-life failures
 • Ensure long-term reliability

Aehr’s systems (like FOX platform) do exactly that.

Aehr is important to optics/photonics because:
 • Photonics chips are still semiconductor devices needing rigorous testing
 • Reliability is mission-critical in AI + data centers
 • AEHR provides wafer-level burn-in, which:
 • Improves yield
 • Prevents failures
 • Enables scaling

As optical interconnects replace copper in AI infrastructure, AEHR quietly becomes a critical enabler behind the scenes

Think of the stack:

 2. Foundries → manufacture them

 • yields drop
 • costs rise
 • reliability risks increase
 • adoption slows

 • Gatekeeper of reliability
 • Accelerator of mass adoption
 • Cost reducer via wafer-level testing

Simple Analogy:

Silicon photonics = high-performance race cars
Aehr = stress-testing every engine before the race

As silicon photonics scales (especially with AI),
AEHR becomes more valuable—not less

$AAOI selloff today looks like the market reacting to the ATM expansion, but the context matters. the company increased the equity distribution agreement from $250M to $500M. That means dilution risk and that’s why the stock dropped.

But look at the timing.

AAOI just announced its first volume order for 1.6T data center transceivers from a hyperscale customer. If demand for 1.6T optics is real and accelerating, raising capital now to fund capacity, inventory and customer ramp actually makes sense.

They already raised about $250M, and the first batch of shares averaged roughly $100+ per share, which suggests institutions were willing to buy stock during the run.

$AAOI Trade Idea - If you're looking to buy AAOI cheaper consider a Sell to Open Put. 
 
Let's say you have $10K cash available and you'd like to buy AAOI in the next week or two for under $100. You place a Sell to Open Put order, 1 contract (100 shares) for the 3/27 expiration - $95 Strike. If your order executes you collect $600 today and agree to buy AAOI for $95 a share until 3/27 (in essence you paid $89 a share). If the share price never gets below $95 you keep the $600 premium.  
 
The risk is the share price could go under $95 and you still are obligated to buy at that price. Same sort of risk you take by buying in the open market now and HODLing.

1. The 100G Hyper-Scale Cycle (2017–2018) 
In 2017, AAOI was a market darling, guiding for massive annual growth driven by 100G transceivers for Amazon. 
 
The Guidance: Management signaled sustained high-margin demand throughout 2017 and into 2018. 
 
The Failure: In Q3 2017, the company abruptly slashed guidance due to a "temporary" slowdown from its largest customer (Amazon). During the post-pandemic supply chain recovery, AAOI repeatedly guided for a significant annual ramp in its Cable TV (CATV) segment. 
 
The Guidance: Management projected that the rollout of DOCSIS 3.1 and 4.0 upgrades would drive a major revenue "inflection point" starting in late 2021.

•  Step 3:  S_{\text{post}} = 75,198,817 + 2,818,760 \approx 78,017,577 
•  Step 4:  V_{\text{post}} = \frac{977,385,000}{78,017,577} \approx 12.53 

This results in an increase of about $2.79 per share for existing shareholders compared to the pre-offering $9.74 (dilution is less severe at higher prices since fewer shares are issued). 

For new investors, the dilution would be $88.68 - $12.53 = $76.15 per share.

This is the most critical factor for the stock price.

$AAOI Did you know that you can be paid to buy shares of AAOI at a lower price? Sounds crazy but it's true. 
 
https://www.investopedia.com/terms/s/selltoopen.asp 
 
You can "Sell to Open" a PUT at, for example, a $25 Strike Price expiring on January 2. Let's say you sell 1 contract for $1 per contract. You would collect $100 now (minus a small commission) and agree to buy 100 shares of AAOI at $25 if the price goes that low by Jan 2nd.  
 
Cons - Share price goes under $25 you still must buy it at $25, but that's essentially the risk of buying the shares at that price anyway, but with the premium you are getting the shares for about $24. 
 
Pros - Share price never reaches $25 by Jan 2 - You keep the premium and don't have to buy the shares. Share price goes higher - You can "Buy to Close" the option at a much lower price premium and keep the difference. Useful if the expiration date is much further out.

Edge AI (On device or near device) computing is the future. BZAI is Edge AI focus technology company

Elon Musk's concept of an "edge node AI" is the idea that personal devices, such as phones, laptops, and cars, will evolve into on-device AI processors. Instead of relying on centralized cloud servers for AI inference, these devices will process data locally, dramatically reducing latency and dependence on network connectivity. 

Why Musk envisions an edge node AI future
Bandwidth limitations: Musk has stated that limitations on bandwidth make it impractical to perform all AI processing server-side. On-device processing is a more efficient and practical solution for a future of ubiquitous AI.

Replacement of traditional software: He predicts a future where a single AI system, like xAI's Grok, will replace conventional apps and operating systems. This AI would generate visuals and audio in real-time, effectively eliminating the need for hardcoded applications. xAI is reportedly developing an AI-only operating system called "Neural OS" (NOS).

Speed and efficiency: Performing AI tasks on-device enables instant responses, eliminating the lag associated with cloud-based processing. This is critical for applications like self-driving cars and robotics that require immediate decision-making.

Privacy and resilience: With on-device AI, sensitive data can be processed locally, reducing privacy concerns associated with sending data to external servers. It also allows AI functionality to work offline.
